title: Recurrent Networks and Sequence Modeling
summary: RNNs, LSTMs, and GRUs for sequential data. Still used in time-series despite Transformers.
key_concepts: RNN: h_t = tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t)
BPTT: backprop through time — vanishing gradients over long sequences
LSTM: forget, input, output gates + cell state
GRU: simplified LSTM with reset and update gates
exercises: Implement an RNN from scratch and show vanishing gradient over 100 steps.
Train a character-level RNN on text and generate samples.
Compare LSTM vs Transformer on a time-series forecasting task.
code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LSTMCell(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size):
        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
        # Combined gate weights
        self.W = nn.Linear(input_size + hidden_size, 4 * hidden_size)

    def forward(self, x, state):
        h, c = state
        combined = torch.cat([x, h], dim=1)
        gates = self.W(combined)
        f, i, g, o = gates.chunk(4, dim=1)  # forget, input, cell, output

        f = torch.sigmoid(f)
        i = torch.sigmoid(i)
        g = torch.tanh(g)
        o = torch.sigmoid(o)

        c_new = f * c + i * g     # forget old + write new
        h_new = o * torch.tanh(c_new)  # output gated cell state
        return h_new, c_new
thinking_questions: Why does the LSTM cell state solve the vanishing gradient problem?
When would you still use an LSTM over a Transformer?
